course_description:
Behavioural study of buyer behaviour, cultural and social factors, personality, learning, attitude theory, brand loyalty, diffusion of innovations, models of consumer behaviour.

This course provides a theoretical basis for understanding the field of consumer behaviour, by drawing on the contributing disciplines of social psychology, sociology, economics and cultural anthropology. It develops an awareness of the consumer as a central driver of marketing strategy and encourages the appreciation of theories and models of consumer behaviour as tools of conceptual analysis. It does this by reviewing the external influences on behaviour (culture, social class, and reference group) and internal individual differences (personality, motivations, emotions, memory, and learning) and by showing how these factors are manifested in buyer attitudes, perceptions, decision-making, and consumption rituals and experiences. The course encourages students to apply theoretical concepts to explain behaviour in a range of marketing situations. It also encourages students to integrate these concepts into a framework that encompasses both micro (individual consumer) and macro (market) environments, as well as factors and trends.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Sustainable Development Goals - UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of the United Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME) . As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through the Sustainable Development Goals . The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.

course_aims: This course aims to acquaint you with consumer behaviour theory and its application in marketing practice. Throughout the semester you will (1) increase your understanding of the theoretical underpinnings and practical implications of consumer behaviour to business and society; (2) make more informed choices about appropriate consumer theories, research approaches and ethical practices for making marketing decisions, and (3) participate in a forum where you will be encouraged to explore and critique consumer research and current marketing approaches in the marketplace.

learning_outcomes.description: Define, illustrate and critically analyse the cognitive, behavioural and cultural theories that inform an understanding of consumer behaviour.

Appreciate and evaluate the range of theories that can be applied to understanding aspects of consumers values, personality, learning, buying decision-making, gender, lifestyles, social class and consumption culture.

Complete a research project that demonstrates creativity and an ability to apply theory, and present research that informs a contemporary understanding of consumer behaviour relevant to marketing management.
Demonstrate effective communication abilities in making consumer and marketing decisions.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1: Poster Presentation
assessment_details.task_description: In this individual assessment , students will assume the role of a marketer seeking to influence a specific consumer behaviour. They will draw on consumer behaviour theory and academic literature to explain why the selected behaviour occurs and to propose evidence-based, actionable strategies to influence it. This assessment will be submitted as an A3-sized poster/page. Students are required to submit a soft copy of their posters by the due date and display and present them to peers during Teaching Week 7 tutorials (Tutorial 6). Complete details will be provided in lectures and tutorials. To complete this assessment, students will be required to undertake the following tasks: Identify and describe a specific consumer behaviour a student aims to influence as a marketer (e.g., safe driving, reducing overconsumption). Explain why consumers engage in this behaviour, drawing on academic literature to demonstrate an informed understanding of the psychological, social, or cultural drivers of the behaviour. (Primary data collection is not required.) Apply at least two relevant consumer behaviour concepts or theories covered in this course to explain or influence the behaviour. Additional theories may be used if relevant. Propose 2–3 actionable, theory-driven strategies that marketers could use to influence the selected behaviour. Briefly describe possible business, social and policy implications resulting from a change in this behaviour. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Your poster is to be submitted to Blackboard either on or before the due date.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note that the extension for this assessment is for submitting the soft copy of the poster, but not for the presentation.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
10% Late Penalty applies to submitted material only. Late submissions are not accepted for in-class presentations. Failure to present at the scheduled time will result in a mark of zero for the presentation portion of this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 2: Research Report (Group project)
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment is a group project (5 students per group). In this assessment, students will conduct research on a chosen consumer behaviour phenomenon and prepare a report. Students will be required to do the following tasks for this assignment: Choose a topic that one of the group members selected for Assessment 1 or choose a completely new topic. Conduct research to understand the selected consumer behaviour topic and identify and justify three research questions related to the selected topic that the group aims to answer. Prepare a research design to conduct interviews. Analyse and report the results of research. Provide a critical discussion of research findings by integrating at least one concept/theory for each research question. Provide practical implications of research. Peer Review: Group members are expected to contribute equally and fulfil the agreed tasks. If disagreements regarding team members' contributions cannot be resolved, students must bring the issue to the attention of their Course Coordinator as early as possible. All students will be required to undertake a Peer Review process, which can influence the marks of those who do not contribute to group work. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Assessment 3: Final Exam
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment is a closed-book examination conducted during the end-of-semester examination period. The exam will comprise multiple-choice questions and short-answer questions, designed to assess students’ understanding and application of key consumer behaviour concepts covered in the course. Further information about the exam format will be provided on the course Blackboard site and in class towards the end of the semester. AI Statement This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Lecture 1 - Introduction to consumer behaviour This lecture will provide an overview of the course and an understanding of consumer behaviour. Note: There are no tutorials in Week 1. Tutorials will commence from Week 2.

Lecture 2 - Contemporary perspectives on consumer behaviour and briefing on Assessment 1 Understanding the contemporary perspectives on consumer behaviour. Briefing on Assessment 1: Poster Presentation.
Tutorial 1 - Introduction to consumer behaviour An overview of the course and assessments
Lecture 3 - Decision-making & involvement Understanding the consumer decision-making process and the role of consumer involvement.
Tutorial 2 - Contemporary perspectives on consumer behaviour and Assessment 1 Group discussions on mini case studies based on Week 2 lecture content and Assessment 1 overview.
Lecture 4 - Learning & memory Understanding theories of learning (e.g., classical conditioning and operant conditioning) and how memory functions.
Tutorial 3 - Decision-making & involvement Group discussion on mini cases and a short question based on Week 3 lecture.

Lecture 5 - Information session for Assessment 2: Research Report (Group project) This lecture will provide a detailed overview of Assessment 2: Research Report (group project), including guidelines for all sections, the marking rubric/criteria, and information on report structure and group formation.
Tutorial 4 - Learning & memory Group discussion on mini case studies relevant to the Week 4 lecture.
Lecture 6 - Perceptual process Exploring the process of perception and related concepts.

Tutorial 5 - Information session for Assessment 2 This tutorial will recap the requirements for Assessment 2 (A2) and address any questions you may have about it. It will also help with group formation for A2. Good Friday Public Holiday - Friday 3 April 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected tutorial classes.
In-Semester Break
Lecture 7 - Attitude & behaviour change Exploring the theories of attitude to understand how attitudes are formed and modified.

Tutorial 6 - Poster Presentations (Assessment 1) In this tutorial, students will bring a printed copy of their poster (printed on A3-sized paper) and display and present it during their respective tutorials.
Lecture 8 - Personality (Part I) Understanding personality and its implications for consumer behaviour (Part I of the theories on Personality).
Tutorial 7 - Attitude & behaviour change Group discussion on mini case studies based on the Week 7 lecture.

Lecture 9 - Personality (Part II) and Q&A session for Assessment 2 Understanding consumer personality and brand personality (the second part of the theories of Personality). Q&A session for Assessment 2: Research Report.
Tutorial 8 - Personality (Part I) Group discussion on mini case studies based on the Week 8 lecture.
Lecture 10 - Self & motivation Importance of self, self-product congruence, and motivation in consumer behaviour.

Tutorial 9 - Personality (Part II) and Q&A session for Assessment 2 Group discussion on mini case studies based on the Week 9 lecture and Q&A session for Assessment 2. Labour Day Public Holiday - Monday 4 May 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected tutorial classes.
Lecture 11 - Groups & social processes The role of reference groups, social pressure, and social power in consumer behaviour.
Tutorial 10 - Self & motivation Group discussion on mini case studies based on the Week 10 lecture.

Lecture 12 - Consumer culture & briefing on Assessment 3: Final Exam This lecture will discuss the influence of culture on consumer behaviour and provide a detailed overview of Assessment 3: Final Exam.
Tutorial 11 - Groups & social processes Group discussion on mini case studies based on the Week 11 lecture.
Lecture 13 - Buying patterns and future trends of consumer behaviour (Last week) Discussing future trends in consumer behaviour. Q&A session for Assessment 3

Tutorial 12 - Consumer culture and Q&A session for Assessment 3 This final tutorial will provide a brief reflection on the Week 12 lecture content and address any questions you may have regarding Assessment 3: Final Exam.
